UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile and global preferences within the IronZen application. This entity stores data linked to an external authentication system.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity. This typically corresponds to the user ID from the authentication system.

email.description: The user's email address, often retrieved from the authentication provider.

displayName.description: The user's display name or full name.

avatarUrl.description: URL to the user's profile picture.

themeDefault.description: The user's preferred default theme setting (e.g., 'dark', 'light' or a specific theme ID).

telegramWebhookUrl.description: The webhook URL configured by the user to receive Telegram notifications.

notificationTime.description: The default time of day for global habit notifications.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was last updated.

Habit.title: Habit
Habit.description: Represents an individual habit that a user wants to track, including its customization and tracking settings.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Habit entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile that owns this habit. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Habit)

title.description: The name or short title of the habit (e.g., 'Morning Run', 'Meditation').

description.description: A detailed description or specific goals for the habit (e.g., '5.0 km • Zone 2', '20 Mins • Vipassana').

habitType.description: Defines the type of data to be tracked for this habit. 'boolean' for simple yes/no completion, 'number' for count or quantity, 'duration' for time-based tracking, 'photo' for visual confirmation.

targetValue.description: The target value for habits tracked by number or duration (e.g., '5' for 5km, '20' for 20 minutes).

unitOfMeasure.description: The unit of measurement for habits with 'number' or 'duration' type (e.g., 'km', 'minutes', 'hours', 'pages').

icon.description: The name of the Material Symbol icon representing the habit (e.g., 'directions_run', 'self_improvement').

emoji.description: An emoji representing the habit, offering additional visual flair.

themeColorHex.description: The hexadecimal color code used for theming this specific habit's card and progress visuals.

minDailyValue.description: The minimum acceptable value for a daily log entry for habits of type 'number' or 'duration'.

maxDailyValue.description: The maximum acceptable value for a daily log entry for habits of type 'number' or 'duration'.

reminderTime.description: A specific time of day for this habit's notification, overriding the user's default notification time.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the habit was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the habit was last updated.

archived.description: A flag indicating if the habit is archived (inactive) rather than deleted.

HabitLog.title: HabitLog
HabitLog.description: Records individual daily entries for a habit, tracking user progress over time.

id.description: Unique identifier for the HabitLog entry.

habitId.description: Reference to the Habit this log entry belongs to. (Relationship: Habit 1:N HabitLog)

logDate.description: The specific date for which this habit entry was recorded.

value.description: The logged value for the habit on this date. Its interpretation depends on the parent Habit's 'habitType' (e.g., 'true' for boolean, '5' for a number, '20' for a duration). If habitType is 'photo', this could be 'true'/'false' for completion, with the image URL in 'imageUrl'.

notes.description: Optional notes or remarks for this specific habit log entry.

imageUrl.description: URL to a picture, relevant if the parent habit's 'habitType' is 'photo'.

loggedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the habit log entry was actually recorded in the system.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the habit log entry was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. Access is strictly controlled by the 'userId' matching the authenticated user's UID.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the user, corresponding to Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: A subcollection for habits owned by a specific user. Includes a denormalized 'userId' field within the document for authorization independence, ensuring simple security rules for ownership.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the owning user.

params.name: habitId
params.description: The unique identifier for the habit.

definition.description: A subcollection for daily log entries belonging to a specific habit. For robust authorization independence, each document MUST include a denormalized 'userId' field in addition to 'habitId', allowing rules to verify user ownership directly without 'get()' operations.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier for the owning user (denormalized).

params.name: habitId
params.description: The unique identifier for the parent habit.

params.name: habitLogId
params.description: The unique identifier for the habit log entry (e.g., date-based ID).

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for IronZen prioritizes secure, scalable, and debuggable authorization by strictly adhering to the core design principles, especially Authorization Independence and Structural Segregation.

1.  **User Profiles (`/users/{userId}`)**: User profile data is stored directly under the `/users` top-level collection, with each document ID (`userId`) matching the Firebase Authentication UID. This follows the 'Private Data' access model, ensuring direct path-based authorization (`request.auth.uid == userId`) and making rules clear and simple. All documents in this collection share the same security posture (owned by the user).

2.  **Habits (`/users/{userId}/habits/{habitId}`)**: Each habit is stored as a subcollection under its owning user's profile. This implements the 'Hierarchical Paths for User-Owned Data' pattern. The `Habit` document explicitly contains a `userId` field, which matches the `{userId}` wildcard in the path. This redundancy, though seemingly minor, is crucial for Authorization Independence. It allows security rules to verify ownership by checking `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` directly on the `Habit` document, eliminating the need for `get()` calls to the parent `UserProfile` document. This also supports Query-Able Paths (QAPs), as `list` operations on `/users/{userId}/habits` are inherently scoped to the authenticated user.

3.  **Habit Logs (`/users/{userId}/habits/{habitId}/habitLogs/{habitLogId}`)**: Daily habit entries are nested further as a subcollection under their respective `Habit` documents. This continues the 'Nested 1:N' hierarchical path structure. For **CRITICAL Authorization Independence**, each `HabitLog` document **MUST** contain a denormalized `userId` field (matching the owning user's ID) in addition to its `habitId`. While the provided `HabitLog` schema does not explicitly list `userId`, its inclusion is fundamental for creating robust and efficient security rules. Without this denormalization, rules for `HabitLog` would be forced to perform a `get()` operation on the parent `Habit` document to verify ownership, directly violating the Authorization Independence mandate, which impacts performance, cost, and debuggability, and prevents atomic operations involving both parent and child.

**QAPs (Query-Able Paths) Support**:
*   Listing a user's `UserProfile` is direct and secure via the `/users/{userId}` path.
*   Listing all `Habit` documents for a specific user is secure via `/users/{userId}/habits`. The `userId` in the path ensures that only the authenticated user can retrieve their habits.
*   Listing `HabitLog` documents for a specific habit of a specific user is secure via `/users/{userId}/habits/{habitId}/habitLogs`. The hierarchical path provides the necessary filtering. For collection group queries across all `habitLogs` (e.g., to build a unified dashboard view or heat map for a user), the denormalized `userId` field in the `HabitLog` document is essential. Rules could then check `allow list: if request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId;` ensuring security without path restrictions.

This structure ensures clear ownership, simplified security rules, and efficient data retrieval, adhering to all mandated design principles.
